items.q: Why can transition metals show variable oxidation states?
items.correct: The 4s and 3d orbitals are similar in energy, so different numbers of electrons can be removed without a large energy cost.
items.misconception: Transition metals have variable oxidation states because they have many shells of electrons.
items.random_wrong: Transition metals have variable oxidation states because they can gain protons easily.
items.why: Students attribute it vaguely to 'lots of electrons' rather than the specific 4s/3d energy proximity that makes successive ionisations comparable in cost.

items.q: At which electrode are positive ions reduced during electrolysis?
items.correct: At the cathode (the negative electrode) — positive ions gain electrons there.
items.misconception: Positive ions are reduced at the anode because that's where positive things go.
items.random_wrong: Positive ions are oxidised at the cathode by losing protons.
items.why: Students associate 'positive' with 'attracted to positive' or get muddled by the sign conventions and switch the electrodes.

items.q: Does a catalyst affect the position of equilibrium?
items.correct: No — it speeds up both the forward and reverse reactions equally, so equilibrium is reached faster but at the same position.
items.misconception: A catalyst shifts the equilibrium towards the products.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst increases the yield by being consumed in the forward reaction only.
items.why: Students associate 'catalyst' with 'helps the reaction' and assume that means 'pushes towards products' rather than 'speeds up both directions equally'.
The answer examiners want is: Loss of e⁻. “Gain of e⁻” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In H₂SO₄, S has OS of:
items.why: It's +6. “+4” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: OS of Cr in K₂Cr₂O₇: (K = +1, O = -2)
items.why: Exact answer: 6. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
